{{Short description="Integral of a force over a time interval, equal to the change in momentum}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Impulse (physics)
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Force]], [[Momentum]], [[Newton's laws of motion]]
| symbol = J
| SI_unit = [[newton second]] (N·s)
| dimension = M L T⁻¹
}}

In [[classical mechanics]], '''impulse''' (symbol <math>\mathbf{J}</math> or <math>\mathbf{I}</math>) is the [[integral]] of a [[force]] <math>\mathbf{F}</math> over the time interval <math>\Delta t</math> during which it acts:

:<math>\mathbf{J} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathbf{F} \, dt</math>

For a constant force:
:<math>\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{F} \Delta t</math>

The [[SI unit]] of impulse is the [[newton second]] (N·s), which is dimensionally equivalent to [[kilogram]]-metre per second (kg·m/s).

== Impulse–momentum theorem ==
Impulse is equal to the change in [[momentum]] of the object on which the force acts:

:<math>\mathbf{J} = \Delta\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_i = m\mathbf{v}_f - m\mathbf{v}_i</math>

This is derived from [[Newton's second law]]:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt} \implies \int\mathbf{F}\,dt = \Delta\mathbf{p}</math>

== Significance ==
The impulse concept is especially useful when:
* The force varies with time in a complex way (only the integral matters)
* Forces act over very short time intervals (collisions, impacts)
* The exact force profile is unknown, but the total change in momentum is known

== Impulsive forces ==
Large forces acting over very short time intervals are called ''impulsive forces''. Examples:
* Bat hitting a baseball (~0.001 s)
* Car crash impact
* Rocket engine ignition
* Hammer striking a nail

During an impulsive interaction, non-impulsive forces (such as [[gravity]]) can often be neglected due to their relatively small magnitude.

== Variable mass systems ==
For systems with variable mass (e.g., a [[rocket]] expelling fuel), the impulse is related to the [[thrust]] and exhaust velocity:
:<math>T = v_e \frac{dm}{dt}</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Force]]
* [[Momentum]]
* [[Newton's laws of motion]]
* [[Work (physics)]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Force]]
[[Category:Physical quantities]]
